are_you_ok.title: Chicago Haitians face uncertainty as TPS protections set to expire this Friday
are_you_ok.summary: TPS expiration directly affects a large local Chicago population's status/safety - broad local population check-in.
are_you_ok.description:
Chicago is home to one of the largest Haitian communities in the United States.

After the Department of Homeland Security extended the Temporary Protected Status (TPS) deadline to this Friday, July 24, Haitians are fearful there won't be a second extension.

At 7:30 p.m. July 23-25 and 3 p.m. July 26 at Steppenwolf’s 1700 Theater, 1700 N. Halsted St. Tickets: $33. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://steppenwolf.org/lookout" target="_blank" ><u>steppenwolf.org/lookout</u></a>.</li><li>The Valkyrie Ensemble, a feminist opera collective, presents an all-femme production of Handel’s opera<b> “Acis & Galatea,”</b> which tells the story of the water nymph Galatea (soprano Jessica Adkins) and her mortal love Acis (soprano Ana Marie Miranda). At 7:30 p.m. July 24-25 at Chicago Danztheatre, 1650 W. Foster Ave. Tickets: $35, $55. Visit <a class="Link" href="https://events.humanitix.com/acis-and-galatea" target="_blank" ><u>humanitix.com/acis-and-galatea</u></a>.</li></ul><h3>Comedy</h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-2458a153-847e-11f1-808a-5f722232d9e5"><li>The four-day <b>iO Fest,</b> Chicago’s largest improv festival, features improv, sketch comedy and standup from more than 130 acts plus workshops and talkbacks with headliners. Among the performers are ¡Escandalo!, Claire + Kim, Jackbox Games, 3Peat, Sand, Armando, Improvised Shakespeare Company, Baby Wants Candy, Astronomy Club, Comedy Dance Chicago, and more. From July 23-26 at iO Theater, 1501 N. Kingsbury St. Ticket prices vary. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://ioimprov.com" target="_blank" ><u>ioimprov.com</u></a>.</li></ul><h3>Dance</h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-2458a154-847e-11f1-808a-5f722232d9e5"><li><b>Chicago Dance Crash</b> presents the world premiere of choreographer Annie Franklin’s “expo/sd,” which investigates how traditional street and contemporary dance forms can exist in conversation while pushing dances to embrace the visceral, the intimate and the imperfect. Also performed are Elijah Motley’s “A 4 Bar Loop” and Cameron McKinney’s “Fall From Grace.” At 7 p.m. July 24-25 at Ruth Page Center for the Arts, 1016 N. Dearborn St. Tickets: $25. conversation_starters.title: Rescued belugas have arrived in Chicago. The move is unprecedented — and controversial
conversation_starters.summary: Marquee local institution (Shedd Aquarium) receives rescued beluga whales in an 'unprecedented' move — widely known Chicago story, light/novel and work-safe. You could open with: 'Did you see Shedd took in those rescued beluga whales? Apparently it's a first-of-its-kind rescue.'
conversation_starters.description: <p>In special steel containers marked “live animals, this side up,” the mammoth sea creatures arrived in Chicago in the pre-dawn hours Tuesday.</p><p>It was the first phase of a beluga whale rescue mission Shedd Aquarium staff call “urgent” and “unprecedented.”</p><p>Lori Marino, an animal rights advocate, says it never should have come to this.</p><p>She and others like her envisioned the belugas making their home in a cordoned-off cove along Nova Scotia’s northeastern shore.</p><p>“It’s the best possible solution. Even though it’s still captivity, we are giving them back as much as we possibly can — what was taken from them in the entertainment industry,” said Marino, co-founder and president of the planned $15 million Whale Sanctuary Project in Port Hilford Bay, Nova Scotia.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f90000" name="image-f90000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/de714cf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+131/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2F12%2Fdf011b4e43fd8c92d972fa7ddd43%2Fbeluga-rescue-821a3554.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/23b2fcd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+131/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2F12%2Fdf011b4e43fd8c92d972fa7ddd43%2Fbeluga-rescue-821a3554.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/114d6dd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+131/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2F12%2Fdf011b4e43fd8c92d972fa7ddd43%2Fbeluga-rescue-821a3554.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Belugas arrive at Shedd Aquarium on Tuesday, July 21, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/114d6dd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+131/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2F12%2Fdf011b4e43fd8c92d972fa7ddd43%2Fbeluga-rescue-821a3554.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/014e36b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+131/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2F12%2Fdf011b4e43fd8c92d972fa7ddd43%2Fbeluga-rescue-821a3554.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/114d6dd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+131/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2F12%2Fdf011b4e43fd8c92d972fa7ddd43%2Fbeluga-rescue-821a3554.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Four belugas, all female, arrived Tuesday morning at Shedd Aquarium.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>©Shedd Aquarium/Heidi Zeiger</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Instead, four of the 30 belugas at the shuttered Marineland aquatic park in Niagara Falls, Ontario, were settling in at Shedd Tuesday. Named Acadia, Lillooet, Osiris and Sierra, the Chicago belugas are all females. Shedd officials said they were chosen to move together “because of their preexisting bonds with one another.” The youngest is 21 years old, while the oldest of the four is 27.</p><p>“While the whales have arrived safely, a transport effort of this magnitude is difficult, and the aquarium cautions that experts expect to learn more about the rescued belugas’ health and current condition in the coming days,” Shedd announced in a news release.</p><p>Some Canadian animal rights advocates have been sounding the alarm for years about animal welfare concerns at Marineland, including cramped tanks and poor water quality. They argue the whales shouldn’t be going from one “concrete tank” to another, and exporting them to Shedd and other aquariums outside the country may even violate Canadian law.</p><p>“The real concern that I and others have is ensuring that Shedd and the other aquariums aren’t going to breed the belugas,” said Camille Labchuk, executive director of Animal Justice, a Toronto-based animal law advocacy group. “If they breed them, that will completely undermine Canada’s national laws, which intended for this generation of whales to be the last suffering in captivity.”</p><p>Johnny Ford, a Shedd spokesman, said "right now, the entire focus of our animal care team is on facilitating a safe rescue."</p><p>But he didn't rule out the Marineland belugas being bred at some point.</p><p>“In the future, if belugas from Marineland were to breed at the aquarium, it would be no different than the way that any other species that is managed by Shedd would breed and that is guided by scientific research, by [Association of Zoos and Aquariums] accreditation standards. … It’s data-informed and it’s always done responsibly.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div alt="A group of experts from Marineland and several US aquariums prepared "Bertie Botts." a beluga whale, for transport from Marineland in Niagara Falls, Ontario, on Monday, July 20, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/64600c7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+264/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2F4d%2F18cc9bbd462db68faa3a7122fb78%2Fap26202018809221.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1ba2251/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+264/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2F4d%2F18cc9bbd462db68faa3a7122fb78%2Fap26202018809221.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/64600c7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5000x2806+0+264/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd8%2F4d%2F18cc9bbd462db68faa3a7122fb78%2Fap26202018809221.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A group of experts from Marineland and several US aquariums load “Bertie Botts.” a beluga whale, into a sling to. be transported from from Marineland in Niagara Falls, Ontario, on Monday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Carlos Osorio/The Canadian Press via AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>A Canadian theme park shutters </h3><p>For generations, families flocked to Marineland just south of Niagara Falls, drawn to the sprawling park for its zoo, rides and shows featuring leaping orcas, twirling dolphins and trainers kissing belugas.</p><p>“Everyone loves Marineland,” went the park’s catchy jingle.</p><p>Not everyone. The park has drawn protesters for years, accusing Marineland of poor treatment of animals. A total of more than 20 whales and dolphins have died at the park since 2019, according to media reports. Changing cultural attitudes about keeping animals in captivity also contributed to dwindling attendance.</p><p>Labchuk last stepped inside Marineland in 2021.</p><p>“They had dolphins spinning in the air to the [Lou Bega] song, ‘Mambo No. 5.’ That tells you all you need to know,” she said.</p><p>The park closed permanently to the public in 2024. Numerous online drone videos show the belugas languishing in grubby tanks.</p><p>The question then was how best to care for the remaining animals?</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-060000" name="image-060000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c255f6c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F08%2F64%2Fcccd068c455d9e7054af6472e9f8%2Fbeluga-rescue-1482.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ea04882/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F08%2F64%2Fcccd068c455d9e7054af6472e9f8%2Fbeluga-rescue-1482.png 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2ab3300/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F08%2F64%2Fcccd068c455d9e7054af6472e9f8%2Fbeluga-rescue-1482.png" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Steel containers containing beluga whales arrived at the Shedd Aquarium before dawn on Tuesday, July 21, 2026. The whales were being transported to the Shedd from the shuttered Marineland park in Niagara Falls, Canada. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2ab3300/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F08%2F64%2Fcccd068c455d9e7054af6472e9f8%2Fbeluga-rescue-1482.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/25e243f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F08%2F64%2Fcccd068c455d9e7054af6472e9f8%2Fbeluga-rescue-1482.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2ab3300/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F08%2F64%2Fcccd068c455d9e7054af6472e9f8%2Fbeluga-rescue-1482.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The steel containers containing the beluga whales from Marineland arrived in the pre-dawn hours on Tuesday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Shedd Aquarium/Brenna Hernandez</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Marino, a neuroscientist and New York University professor, is among a group of animal welfare advocates who, about 11 years ago, began searching for a coastal sanctuary to take up to a dozen captive whales. It would be the first whale sanctuary in North America.</p><p>The group settled on a wooded cove in northeastern Nova Scotia, leasing from the Canadian government an area of coastal water the equivalent of about 100 acres. A wall of netting would prevent the whales from venturing into the open ocean.</p><p>“Having lived in tanks — most of them have been born in tanks — they’re missing all the skills they would need to feed themselves, to form a social group [in open water],” Marino said. “It would be disastrous.”</p><p>Here, harbor seals glide through its slate-blue waters and rock crab scuttle across its sandy bottom. On the shore, black bear and white-tailed deer roam through stands of white spruce and balsam fir.</p><p>The group broke ground on the sanctuary several weeks ago and has raised from private donations and foundations about half of the $15 million they say they need to open.</p><p>Labchuk, a whale sanctuary supporter, said the project would be further along with help from the Canadian federal government.</p><p>“We’ve been telling them for close to a decade that they should be supporting this and that hasn’t materialized,” Labchuk said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div have is ensuring that Shedd and the other aquariums aren’t going to breed the belugas,” she said.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Camille Labchuk</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Meanwhile, Marineland applied for permits in September 2025 to have the whales shipped to a theme park in China. Joanne Thompson, Canada’s minister of Fisheries, denied the permits, saying the transfer was not in the best interests of the whales.</p><p>Marineland promptly wrote back to Thompson and, citing Marineland’s “critical financial state,” said: “Our only options at this point are to relocate the whales or face the devastating decision of euthanasia.”</p><p>The Sun-Times tried to reach representatives from Marineland for comment but had not heard back as of late Tuesday afternoon.</p><p>Earlier this year, Marineland and Canada’s Accredited Zoos and Aquariums asked Shedd, among others, to investigate rescuing the belugas, Shedd has said.</p><p>And on Monday, four of the 30 whales were hoisted out of the water in special slings and loaded into containers before being flown out of Canada to Chicago, according to Canadian media reports.</p><p>Four belugas, all female, arrived Tuesday morning.</p><p>“Currently, the animal care teams at  Shedd are working around-the-clock, looking after the rescued whales, providing immediate care and acclimating the animals. For now, caretakers are moving at each individual whale’s pace, and the rescued belugas are not viewable by the public,” Shedd said Tuesday.</p><p>The newcomers join Shedd’s existing group of eight belugas, which can grow up to 18 feet long and weigh about 3,000 pounds. In the wild, they can live for up to 90 years.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-3e0000" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Beluga whale “Sierra” is transported by a group of experts from Marineland and several U.S. aquariums onto a truck at Marineland in Niagara Falls, Ontario, on Monday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Carlos Osorio/The Canadian Press, distributed by the Associated Press</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Possible violation of Canadian law</h3><p>But Marino, Labchuk and other animal welfare advocates say none of the belugas should be shipped out without first having, at the very least, assurances from the receiving aquariums that they won’t breed the animals.</p><p>A 2019 Canadian law, among other things, bans breeding whales in captivity on Canadian soil. The government’s own policy says an application for export permits must show a commitment from the receiving facility that it “will not breed or impregnate the cetacean being exported from Canada.”</p><p>“The government’s own policies say they should not be exported if they are going to be bred, yet the government seems to have [stepped back] from that position,” Labchuk said. “They are not confirming to us that they are putting that condition on the permits.”</p><p>In January, Thompson, the Canadian fishery minister, met with Canadian reporters and was asked about approving the export permits without including a provision that they not be used for breeding in captivity.</p><p>“My confidence in this is that it includes all of the [Marineland] belugas and the dolphins,” Thompson said. “The sites are accredited — the highest standards for animal welfare in the world.”</p><p>Labchuk said her organization hasn’t ruled out legal action. The Canadian law states marine mammals should be exported only when it’s in their “best interests.”</p><p>“It’s a pretty novel test under the law, and that goes for really anywhere around the planet,” Labchuk said. “Usually when we look at animals, what’s important under the law is the interests of the people who own them.”</p><p>Marino, meanwhile, acknowledges the U.S. receiving aquariums, which also include Georgia Aquarium, SeaWorld San Antonio and SeaWorld San Diego, offer a better life than Marineworld.</p><p>“But this is not what they deserve,” she said. “They deserve a very different life in a sanctuary where they can actually have the ability to thrive.”</p>

conversation_starters.title: Rainbow Cone marks 100th anniversary serving up its signature sliced and stacked treat
conversation_starters.summary: Rainbow Cone hitting 100th anniversary is a beloved Chicago institution milestone with citywide events - great local conversation piece. You could open with: Did you know Rainbow Cone is turning 100 this year? Have you ever tried the five-flavor cone?
conversation_starters.description: <p>To the uninitiated, a Rainbow Cone with its five colorful ice creams stacked in a seemingly gravity-defying way might seem like a recent Instagram-inspired creation. But Chicagoans, especially those on the South Side, know that the Original Rainbow Cone shop and its photogenic namesake are local institutions that predate social media — along with cellphones, computers, the first fully electronic television, microwaves and a host of other modern-day conveniences.</p><p>Launched in August 1926 in a small shack at 92nd Street and Western Avenue, Rainbow Cone turns 100 this year with a slew of events (see below) to mark the celebration. Activities around the city include an immersive experience at the Museum of Ice Cream Chicago and pop-ups at Lookingglass Theatre and Lurie Children’s Hospital.</p><p>Back when Joe and Katherine Sapp started the business, Beverly was nothing like it is today. The couple saw potential in the area that was dominated by dirt roads and dotted with apple orchards and summer cottages. A cemetery across the street proved to be beneficial. </p><p>“Sunday was their biggest day of the week,” said Lynn Sapp, who was the third-generation owner of Rainbow Cone. “People would go to the cemetery to visit loved ones, and they would stop at Rainbow on their way back to the city.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>A car mechanic by trade, Joe was inspired to open Rainbow Cone for a simple reason. “He always loved ice cream and was fascinated by it,” said Sapp. The five flavors of the cone, originally 12 cents, were a nod to the popularity of the era’s three-flavor Neapolitan ice cream.</p><p>Rather than scoops, the Rainbow Cone’s ice creams were sliced into slabs using a special tool Joe created, a clever eye-catching design element that also helped hold it together. The ice creams’ order is the same now as it was then: chocolate, strawberry, Palmer House (vanilla with walnuts and cherries), pistachio and orange sherbet on top.</p><p>Not too long after Rainbow Cone opened, the Depression started. Sapp credits her grandmother with keeping the business afloat. “Katherine was the businesswoman behind him and threw her nickel around like a sewer cover,” she said, adding that her grandfather was “a genius of a man who could fix anything.”</p><p>Joe’s handiwork could be seen all over Rainbow Cone, especially when it moved across the street to a larger location where the couple lived on the second floor of the two-story building painted pink, Katherine’s favorite color. There was a mechanical bird he made that would tell customers to move down the line. It didn’t last long as everybody would stop to look at the bird and no one would move.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f00000" name="image-f00000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7f143b3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8a%2Fad%2Fe6eb13e04107ac0d66fa86a20710%2Frainbowcone-260722-7.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7e5d882/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8a%2Fad%2Fe6eb13e04107ac0d66fa86a20710%2Frainbowcone-260722-7.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7750400/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8a%2Fad%2Fe6eb13e04107ac0d66fa86a20710%2Frainbowcone-260722-7.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Lynn Sapp constructs the namesake Rainbow Cone inside the business’ founding location at 9233 S. Western Ave. in the Beverly neighborhood." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7750400/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8a%2Fad%2Fe6eb13e04107ac0d66fa86a20710%2Frainbowcone-260722-7.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/723ee35/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8a%2Fad%2Fe6eb13e04107ac0d66fa86a20710%2Frainbowcone-260722-7.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7750400/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8a%2Fad%2Fe6eb13e04107ac0d66fa86a20710%2Frainbowcone-260722-7.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Lynn Sapp constructs the namesake Rainbow Cone at the Beverly shop. “When it’s a family business, it’s at your dinner table and you live with it,” said Sapp, who started working at Rainbow Cone’s counter when she was 14 years old. Her three older siblings worked there, too.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The mirrors at 92nd are the same mirrors as when Rainbow Cone opened 100 years ago, Sapp says. “It was Al Capone days. The gangsters were alive and well. So, the way the parlor was designed, [her grandfather] put up mirrors all everywhere. He could stand anywhere in the store and by looking in a mirror, he knew exactly who was coming in.”</p><p>During World War II, a shortwave radio Joe built drew in customers with cones in hand to the shop’s back patio eager for updates. “It became an information center for the neighborhood,” Sapp said.</p><p>In the ’60s, Sapp’s parents, Robert and Jean, who lived in a house behind Rainbow Cone, took over the business. Growing up, Sapp, like her three older siblings, worked there. “When it’s a family business, it’s at your dinner table and you live with it,” said Sapp, who started working at Rainbow Cone’s counter when she was 14, calling it “an honor and a privilege” despite the long days and hard work.</p><p>“When you’d hear people's stories like, ‘Oh my gosh, I came here with my grandparents and I haven't had this in 10 years and it brings back such memories,’ that really helps.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Rainbow Cone layers are and always have been, from bottom to top of the cone: chocolate, strawberry, Palmer House (vanilla with walnuts and cherries), pistachio and orange sherbet.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>It also didn’t hurt living behind an ice cream parlor. “We always had ice cream all around us,” she said.</p><p>While her siblings went on to careers in other fields, Sapp stayed on and took over the business in 1987. First order of business was doing away with the fancy white outfits the staff wore, typical of ice cream parlors back then, and swapping in T-shirts.</p><p>Rainbow’s menu had been through some changes, growing and contracting over the years. Sapp put some items back — think sundaes, shakes and floats — and added her own: ice cream cakes and rolls. Pints and quarts followed. The Rainbow Cone’s ice cream recipes, however, stayed the same beyond changes due to new health regulations, such as the pasteurization of nuts. And the namesake Rainbow Cone is still the top seller.</p><p>One element of her role that Sapp particularly liked was working with the young staff. “It was part of my mission to send them out into the world where they could produce and contribute and make a good living,” she said. “I used to joke with them that if you’re having difficulties with an ice cream cone, you’ve got a problem.”</p><p>Bryan Feltman started working behind the ice cream counter when he was 16 years old. Seventeen years later, he’s still there, albeit now he’s a managing partner. In addition to helping oversee stores, he heads up Rainbow Cone pop-ups like at Taste of Chicago, Lollapalooza and local street fairs.</p><p>“I always knew Rainbow was more than an ice cream shop,” said Feltman, who grew up in Beverly going there as a kid as did his dad. “It’s been passed down from one generation to the next and has become a tradition for birthday parties, home run celebrations, first dates and so many other things. There are a lot of memories and emotions attached to this brand.”</p><p>Feltman has also been on the frontlines dealing with customers who might be hesitant to try the unique ice cream combo. “It’s five flavors that are great on their own but together there’s just something magical about it,” he said. Rainbow Cone offers customers a replacement menu item if they’re not satisfied, but Feltman can count on one hand the number of times he’s had to do that. “It has stood the test of time for 100 years and that alone speaks volumes.”</p><p>After years in the business, Sapp began thinking about winding down her involvement in the company. She also recognized the need to find a company that could embrace technology at Rainbow Cone in a way she wasn’t able to. “The goal was getting as many people to know Rainbow Cone as possible because I think it’s a very unique product, and I’m very proud of it,” she said. </p><p>With no family members interested, she reached out to a neighboring Beverly company, Buona Beef, a multigenerational restaurant chain specializing in Italian beef.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-5c0000" alt="The Rainbow Cone ice cream truck was born during Covid, when restaurants as well as the seasonal festivals were shut down. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/046789d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F68%2Fb3%2F500513c44efcaacdc37b842dcc1f%2Frainbowconetruck-07032024-3.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e6b48f1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F68%2Fb3%2F500513c44efcaacdc37b842dcc1f%2Frainbowconetruck-07032024-3.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/046789d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F68%2Fb3%2F500513c44efcaacdc37b842dcc1f%2Frainbowconetruck-07032024-3.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Rainbow Cone ice cream truck was born during Covid, when restaurants as well as the seasonal festivals were shut down. Johnny Buonavolanto’s cousin came up with the idea of an ice cream truck. They painted an old FedEx truck bright pink with white polka dots and brought it to their Buona Beef parking lots. “We had lines down the block and wrapped around the streets,” said Buonavolanto, director of sales for the Buona Companies.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“She had bigger companies interested but she wanted a Chicago family-owned business to take care of this brand, be a steward of it and honor her family’s legacy,” said Johnny Buonavolanto, director of sales for the Buona Companies. That company was started in 1981 by his grandfather, who, at 90 years old, still comes to the office.</p><p>Since Buona came on board as owners in 2018, Rainbow Cone has grown from two locations (Beverly and Navy Pier) to 30, including ones inside Buona Beef restaurants and in states outside Illinois. Buonavolanto assured Rainbow Cone fans that Sapp is still very much a part of the company and consults on key decisions.</p><p>During the pandemic, when Rainbow Cone and Buona shops as well as seasonal festivals shut down, they scrambled for a way to make money. Buonavolanto’s cousin came up with the idea of an ice cream truck. They painted an old FedEx truck bright pink with white polka dots and brought it to their Buona Beef parking lots. “We had lines down the block and wrapped around the streets,” said Buonavolanto. The addition of more ice cream trucks was inevitable.</p><p>With the anniversary approaching, Sapp is grateful not only to her family but to the city of Chicago and the loyal customers who have embraced Rainbow Cone for 100 years. The uniqueness of the cone itself is a key to the business’ longevity but Sapp also credits her family’s dedication to quality.</p><p>“A lot of people tend to go cheaper, and we protect the quality of the ice cream. My grandpa always said, ‘You have to have a great product and maintain the integrity of it. And you have to make sure it’s in a clean setting and served with a smile.’ It sounds easy but it’s not.”</p><p>She’s also proud of all the memories Rainbow Cone has created.</p><p>“I call it the mini miracle as it’s still around after the Depression and wars and all the other challenges,” Sapp said. “As complicated as people get, it's still a basic ice cream cone and one of the beauties of it is you have to sit back, relax and just enjoy it.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-e30000" name="module-e30000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Taste the Rainbow Cone</h3><br>In honor of its 100th anniversary, Rainbow Cone is marking the occasion with events leading up to its centennial birthday on Aug. 9. Here’s how you can celebrate Chicago’s famous five-layer sweet treat.<br><br><b>Immerse yourself in ice cream</b><br><br>Through Aug. 3, Rainbow Cone and the Museum of Ice Cream Chicago are collaborating on a new two-room exhibition featuring storytelling and sensory discovery. Guests can step into a dreamy space highlighting the Rainbow Cone and enjoy samples of the cold confection before exploring an interactive retrospective chronicling the business’ history.<br><br><b>Coming soon to an event near you</b><br><br>Throughout the summer, Rainbow Cone trucks will pop up at community events across the area. Partners include Lookingglass Theatre Company, Pat Mac’s Pack and Lurie Children’s Hospital of Chicago, alongside additional community organizations and neighborhood initiatives to support nonprofit fundraising efforts and youth programming.<br><br>Dates and locations for the Centennial Summer Surprise Tour will be announced throughout the summer at <a class="Link" href="https://rainbowcone.com/" target="_blank" >rainbowcone.com</a>, and on Instagram and Facebook.<br><br><b>South Side team up</b><br><br>Throughout the season, the first 15,000 fans who enter Rate Field for a White Sox home game will receive a special Five-Flavor Game Card that can be redeemed for a free small cone at participating locations if the Sox score in the fourth inning.<br><br><b>Birthday party on Aug. 9</b><br><br>The big birthday bash takes place where it all began at Rainbow Cone’s original neighborhood of Beverly on Aug. 9, where guests ordering the iconic five-flavor cone can spin a prize wheel for the chance to win everything from free treats and merchandise to a Rainbow Cone Truck party valued at $1,000 for you and all of your ice-cream-loving friends.<br><br><i>-Dorothy Hernandez</i></div> </div><p></p>

you_should_know.title: Migrant crisis cost Chicago $639.6M, and city followed rules in spending it, inspector general says
you_should_know.summary: Inspector general report on the $639.6M cost of Chicago's migrant crisis and finding the city followed spending rules. Major local government-accountability outcome landing on the metro; institutional context. Keep as you-should-know.
you_should_know.description: <p>Chicago spent $639.6 million to house 46,282 people during the migrant crisis that dominated the first 18 months of Mayor Brandon Johnson’s administration, 42% of which came from the city’s corporate fund.</p><p>In one of his first official actions as the city’s chief watchdog, Inspector General David Glockner released what he called an “explainer” on the city's involvement with the migrant crisis, a report that includes final spending numbers and a conclusion that the Johnson administration followed its procurement and payment procedures.</p><p>The numbers tell the story.</p><p>Before the caravan of buses started descending on Chicago, city-owned homeless shelters operated by the Department of Family and Support Services had a capacity of just 3,000 beds. That was nowhere near enough to accommodate the avalanche of migrants — most from Venezuela, with lesser numbers from the Caribbean, Africa, Europe and the Middle East.</p><p>To absorb the new arrivals, the city opened 38 emergency shelters with a combined capacity of 15,000 as well as a landing zone that served as a “centralized intake hub where the city assessed people’s immediate needs” for food and clothing, and placed them in shelters.</p><p>Even that was not enough to accommodate the influx.</p><p>Beginning in April 2023, one month before Johnson took office, Chicago relied heavily on district police stations and O’Hare and Midway Airports to handle the overflow.</p><p>“By October 2023, over 3,000 such people remained in district stations throughout the city, a population larger than the maximum capacity of the pre-existing shelter system,” Glockner’s analysis stated.</p><p>“CPD stated that this use greatly impacted the district stations, which were not designed or equipped to have people living in them. … The Department needed to repair and clean restrooms more often during this time. … Front desk staff complained about the noise levels in lobbies. CPD also had to request special cleaning to prevent bed bug infestations and disease outbreaks.”</p><p>Although the Chicago Police Department did not assign specific staff to handle new arrivals sleeping on police station floors, it did “have to assign additional units to monitor areas surrounding” the largest city-run shelters.</p><p>At O’Hare and Midway, contractors “simply hung drapes on pipes within staging areas” to give new arrivals some semblance of privacy, the report states.</p><p>With so much spending in such a short time, the inspector general’s office set out to determine whether the city followed its own procurement rules and whether the Johnson administration’s migrant spending dashboard had been transparent.</p><p>Glockner concluded the city’s reporting of the $639.6 million of spending from grant funds as well as from the city’s own corporate funds was “accurate and transparent, with only $50,916 in transactions that should have been included in the reported total but were omitted.”</p><p>More than $622 million of the money spent on the migrant mission went to 10 vendors. They were led by Favorite Healthcare Staffing ($400.3 million) and Equitable Social Solutions ($128.9 million).</p><p>And after reviewing a sample of 100 invoices totaling $20.4 million, Glockner found 99.6% of that sample “followed the city’s procurement and invoicing procedures.”</p><p>“Chicago faced a significant humanitarian challenge during the new arrivals mission, resulting in the expenditures of substantial city funds,” Glockner said in a press release that accompanied his analysis.</p><p>“Our review found the city accurately reported this spending and followed established procurement processes — an important foundation for responsible stewardships during urgent, complex operations.”</p><p>Johnson inherited the migrant crisis and got off to a rocky political start, in part because of the difficult choices it forced him to make. One of his first Council meetings included a vote on $51 million in surplus funding for the crisis — after a contentious, “us-versus-them” debate that reduced one alderperson to tears.</p><p>Johnson's first budget included just $150 million in funding he knew would last only until April 1. He low-balled the figure to put pressure on the federal government, but the tactic failed.</p><p>At one point Johnson used $95 million in federal stimulus money to slap an end-of-year Band-Aid on the migrant crisis. But that alienated some alderpersons who control spending and complained they were not consulted.</p><p> During the crisis, Johnson unveiled <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/11/22/23972505/brighton-park-migrant-tent-city-site-polluted-brandon-johnson" >plans for a winterized camp</a> at a contaminated Southwest Side industrial site only to have Gov. JB Pritzker reject the idea amid environmental concerns. The local alderperson, Julia Ramirez, a progressive ally, felt blindsided by the mayor’s plan and was subsequently <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2023/10/19/23924198/tent-city-protesters-attack-ald-julia-ramirez-aide-asylum-seekers-migrants" >physically accosted</a> by furious constituents. The abandoned plan ended up costing Chicago taxpayers nearly $1 million.</p><p>By opening brick-and-mortar shelters at a furious pace, Johnson managed to empty police station lobbies filled with asylum-seekers and return Park District fieldhouses to their intended use. </p>

you_should_know.title: Ald. Ervin proposes 'infrastructure trust' to weigh taking back parking meters
you_should_know.summary: City Council proposal to create an infrastructure trust to buy back the notorious Chicago parking meters. High-profile local government/civic story landing on the metro; institutional context. Keep as you-should-know (a civic-fight story, not a work-safe opener).
you_should_know.description: <p>Ignoring fears that Chicago was “buying high after selling low,” Mayor Brandon Johnson’s most powerful City Council ally on Wednesday forged ahead with his eleventh-hour plan to create a public infrastructure trust to take back the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/05/18/chicagos-parking-meters-could-be-sold-again-under-deal-requiring-city-council-approval" >city’s parking meters.</a></p><p> Budget Committee Chair Jason Ervin (28th) formally introduced his long-shot ordinance that would create a nine-member public trust to pool pension and city funds and match the $2.5 billion winning bid submitted by Stonepeak Partners, a New York investment firm, for the city's parking meters.</p><p>At the City Council meeting Wednesday, the ordinance was shunted off to the Rules Committee by Ald. Anthony Beale (9th), Ervin’s archrival, in an attempt to slow it down. But Ervin is moving full-speed ahead.</p><p>Ervin, whose wife, city Treasurer Melissa Conyears-Ervin, sits on the boards of all four city employee pension funds, said financial and pension experts he refused to identify have volunteered to examine the feasibility of acquiring the 57 years remaining on the parking meter deal Chicagoans love to hate.</p><p>Now that a second extended deadline gives the City Council<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/07/16/chicago-parking-meters-ownership-transfer-city-council-approval-deadline-extended" > until Sept. 30</a> to accept or reject Stonepeak’s offer, those unidentified advisers have the time Ervin believes they will need to decide whether it makes sense to take back the parking meters that former Mayor Richard M. Daley has been accused of all but giving away for a fraction of what future parking revenues were worth.</p><p>The current owner, Chicago Parking Meters LLC, is a consortium that includes Morgan Stanley, Allianz Capital Partners and the Sovereign Wealth Fund of Abu Dhabi. That group has doubled its initial $1.15 billion investment with nearly six decades to go on the deal.</p><p>“It’s very clear that, if Stonepeak is willing to spend $2.5 billion, there is some level of value there to do that,” Ervin told the Chicago Sun-Times. “People in the market think that it’s a worthwhile investment to do. Otherwise, you wouldn’t have such notable people in business pursuing this transaction. Not only did Stonepeak pursue it. Several infrastructure trust individuals pursued this transaction.”</p><p>Civic Federation President Joe Ferguson told the Sun-Times last week that “resurrecting” then-Mayor Rahm Emanuel’s failed infrastructure trust “at this moment … looks like a stall tactic.” </p><p>Ferguson said Ervin is "one of the closest allies of the mayor" and that he "got particularly prickly" when asked whether he was doing the mayor's bidding. </p><p>“This is not the moment to be spitballing all sorts of really complex ideas. ... We’re still at 'sell low and buy high' for something that really is a speculative undertaking," Ferguson said.</p><p>"We don’t know how automobiles and parking [are] going to be working for the next 57 years," Ferguson continued. "We can be pretty sure it’s not gonna work the way it has in the past, or the way it works in the present. So this is a really risky revenue stream, and that revenue stream mostly has to go to simply paying back the debt. Forget about making more money." </p><p>Ervin’s ordinance would establish the trust as an independent corporate entity empowered to acquire and manage infrastructure assets that “produce predictable cash flow, inflation protection, diversification and returns that match long-term public obligations.”</p><p>It would be overseen by a board with nine voting members. Three would be appointed by the mayor. Six would be ex-officio voting members, including the city treasurer.</p><p>Before any acquisition, financing or sale of any asset, the trust “shall engage one or more independent advisors to prepare a report” to the City Council, which must sign off on the acquisition. The report must be delivered to the Council 90 days before a final vote.</p><p>“If the state of Washington’s pension funds are investing in this, why wouldn’t the city of Chicago’s pension funds also be looking at a similar type of investment?” Ervin asked. “If we can help boost our funded ratios in pensions, create a long-term revenue stream for our pensions, this is a win-win. Would we like to have it happen this way? Of course not. But this is the hand we’re dealt." </p><p>Ferguson said the City Council would be better off taking Stonepeak up on its offer to work with the city to reduce the annual "true-up" payment that must be made to the parking meter owner whenever parking spaces are taken out of service for special events and construction projects.</p><p>"See what you can get before you go to this place where you're really just rolling the dice with potentially really huge financial consequences to the city and to the taxpayers," he said.</p>

you_should_know.title: Mexico seeks accountability in Chicago-area ICE death and 16 others. Can it force the U.S. to act?
you_should_know.summary: Mexico's legal pursuit re: local ICE death (Franklin Park) and enforcement climate; institutional/political context on immigration enforcement, not an active check-in event.
you_should_know.description: <p>Mexico’s government has begun <a class="Link" href="https://www.gob.mx/presidencia/prensa/vamos-a-hacer-todo-lo-que-este-en-nuestras-manos-presidenta-sre-inicia-acciones-por-muerte-de-mexicanos-en-custodia-y-operativos-de-ice" target="_blank" ><u>taking legal action</u></a> to seek accountability for the deaths of 17 of its citizens during U.S.Immigration and Customs Enforcement operations — 14 of them in ICE custody, and three during other “arrest operations.” They include Silverio Villegas González, who was shot and killed by an ICE agent during a traffic stop in suburban Franklin Park nearly a year ago.</p><p>Villegas González had lived in the United States since 2007. He was the father of two young children.</p><p>At the time of his death, the Department of Homeland Security said Villegas González had <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/09/16/ice-body-cameras-deadly-shooting-silverio-villegas-gonzalez-chicago" target="_blank" ><u>fled a traffic stop and dragged</u></a> an ICE agent “a significant distance with his car,” seriously injuring the agent. But Franklin Park police body-camera video footage obtained by the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/09/22/ice-officer-injuries-nothing-major-deadly-franklin-park-shooting-mexican-immigrant-chicago-video?utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=092325%20Morning%20Edition&utm_content=092325%20Morning%20Edition+CID_7ddfe7afa69b632582015a9194a33f32&utm_source=cst_campaign_monitor&utm_term=ICE%20officer%20said%20his%20injuries%20were%20nothing%20major%20after%20deadly%20shooting%20near%20Chicago%20video%20shows&tpcc=cst_cm" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago Sun-Times cast doubt on the DHS description of what happened, </u></a>catching the ICE agent saying his injuries were "nothing major," a "knee injury" and "some lacerations" to his hands.</p><p>Mexico’s announcement came days after another ICE agent fatally shot Lorenzo Salgado Araujo during a traffic stop in Houston on July 7. DHS said Salgado Araujo was not the person agents were looking for.</p><p>Mexico President Claudia Sheinbaum has referred to these deaths as “homicides.” As she called for more accountability at a news conference announcing steps her government was taking, Sheinbaum said Mexico “cannot turn a blind eye to the Mexicans who have died.”</p><p>“We are going to do everything within our power,” Sheinbaum said. “What we cannot do is stand by as Mexicans die in ICE operations or detention.”</p><p>Mexico’s effort has three tracks. Mexico officials have filed criminal complaints with state prosecutors in Illinois and elsewhere, and it plans to submit a complaint to the U.S. Department of Justice, seeking independent investigations into the deaths. The Mexican government also issued cease-and-desist letters directing U.S. detention centers to preserve records and called for a formal review by the United Nations Human Rights Council.</p><p>It will be up to U.S. officials to decide whether to investigate or file charges.</p><p>Legal experts and immigration advocates say it’s unclear whether U.S. authorities will cooperate with an investigation but that the process still could put political pressure on the United States to take action and potentially produce evidence that could be used in civil lawsuits.</p><div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ilan Katz, a criminal defense lawyer in Mexico</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Can Mexico force the U.S. to investigate?</h3><p>Mexico can’t compel the United States to investigate or collect evidence, experts say.</p><p>Mexico already is encountering resistance from U.S. officials. Earlier this week, Mexico said the State Department returned a cease-and-desist letter it sent regarding four Mexican citizens who died earlier this year at the Adelanto detention center in San Bernardino County.</p><p>For any sort of investigation into individual deaths, U.S. prosecutors have the freedom to decline to open an investigation or later decide not to file charges in any of these cases, said <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/08/15/cartels-chapo-elchapo-ilan-katz-claudia-sheinbaum-sinaloa-cartel" target="_blank" ><u>Ilan Katz,</u></a> a Mexico-based criminal defense lawyer who is president of the Mexican Bar Foundation.</p><p>“When a government goes to the Department of Justice, it is like any person going to the DOJ," Katz said. "The department has the discretion to act or not act, but it has now been informed of alleged criminal conduct."</p><p>He said he can’t predict what will happen this time.</p><p>“We don’t know, especially because, in the world of Trump, [the] DOJ has become — or at least seems to have become — less independent than it was in the past,” Katz said.</p><p>Tom Ginsburg, a University of Chicago law professor who focuses on constitutional and international law, said even though the Mexican government can’t force the United States to investigate, it can send an important political message to Mexican citizens that their government “is not abandoning them.”</p><p>But he said international law can do little to force the United States to take accountability, and the country has often resisted complying.</p><p>“I know no one’s holding their breath with the Trump administration,” he said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum has called for more accountablity for the deaths of 17 Mexican nationals who dies in ICE custody or during other ICE operations.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>AP Photo/Marco Ugarte</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Waiting for answers</h3><p>Villegas González’s case shows what those limits mean for a family and a community.</p><p>Reyna Torres Mendívil, Mexico’s consul general in Chicago, said Mexico first called for an investigation by the FBI on Sept. 13, 2025, a day after Villegas González died. It also sent a diplomatic request to the U.S. Embassy in Mexico.</p><p>Torres Mendívil said Mexican officials have gotten little information, and ICE has told the consulate that its administrative review of Villegas González’s death remains ongoing.</p><p>A spokesperson for DHS said the “criminal prosecution of the involved ICE officer was declined by the U.S. attorney's office,” but “the ICE Office of Professional Responsibility is continuing its administrative review of the incident.“</p><p>In May, the Illinois State Police <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/05/05/ice-midway-blitz-chicago-trump-silverio-villegas-gonzalez" target="_blank" ><u>said it had opened</u></a> an investigation into the death of Villegas González. No results have been made public.</p><p>Torres Mendívil says attorneys representing the Villegas family are conducting their own inquiry as they consider possible legal action. “They are carrying out a parallel investigation into the circumstances and everything that happened, for whatever legal action may be appropriate,” Torres Mendívil said.</p><p>The consulate cannot represent families directly in U.S. courts, but Torres Mendívil said it can provide money for outside lawyers on a case-by-case basis. “We do provide resources so that attorneys can do it,” she said. “Not in every case. The case has to be viable.”</p><p>In Villegas González’s case, she said the consulate is “assisting the family with the payment of the attorneys” working to reunify his children with relatives.</p><h3><b>Building a record and pressure</b></h3><p>Mexico also has made a formal request to the United Nations to gather information about the deaths, including that of Villegas González, and examine whether the United States met its duties under international law.</p><p>The U.N. could ask U.S. officials what happened, review whether the deaths were properly investigated and call for more information, further investigation or changes in U.S. practices.</p><p>Sarah Dávila, an associate professor of law at University of Illinois Chicago, said the United States has a duty under international human rights law to investigate reports of abuse. But the U.N. Human Rights Council cannot force U.S. agencies to comply.</p><p>“While recommendations from the U.N. High Commissioner for Human Rights are not binding, or findings from United Nations procedures, they carry enormous weight,” Dávila said.</p><p>She said those findings could create an independent record and later be used in U.S. civil or criminal cases.</p><p>The cease-and-desist letters do not carry legal force on their own but serve as a formal “warning” not to destroy or alter evidence and can become important if a case later goes to trial, experts say.</p><p>Ginsburg said Mexico could also make a case before the U.N. that the rights of its citizens were violated under international law. That could include violations of the Vienna Convention on Consular Relations, which requires the agency detaining a foreign national to allow the detainee to have access to their consular officials, who could visit them in detention and help them obtain a lawyer.</p><p>According to Shaw Drake, an assistant professor at the University of New Mexico School of Law, Mexico could have another source of leverage: The United States relies heavily on Mexico to accept deportees, including people from countries other than Mexico.</p><p>“If Mexico refuses to receive those third-country nationals [for deportation], it puts a major wrench in the administration’s current mass deportation efforts,” Drake said.</p><p>This could give Mexico’s demands for accountability greater political weight, he said, if the country chooses to use it.</p><p>Artemio Arreola, political director of the Illinois Coalition for Immigrant and Refugee Rights said even though he thinks Mexico’s legal pursuits are mainly political and diplomatic, investigations are still important because families deserve to know exactly how their relatives died, particularly when evidence has contradicted official accounts.</p><p>“These investigations are even more important because, in many of these cases, the official accounts have turned out to be false,” he said. “Authorities have put forward narratives that later evidence has contradicted. We have to know exactly how these deaths occurred.”</p>
